title: Translation and Interpretation Services

alternatives.name: Translation/localization-led language-service provider
inclusion_criteria: U.S. business classified in NAICS 541930 and estimated to have $1 million to $10 million of EBITDA.

More than 50% of annual language-service revenue is generated by converting or adapting written material, including document translation, localization, terminology, editing, or closely related written-language quality services.
The business may also provide interpreting, but written translation and localization remain the majority revenue model.
exclusion_criteria: Businesses with more than 50% of annual language-service revenue from live spoken-language or sign-language interpreting.
Balanced providers for which neither written translation/localization nor live interpreting exceeds 50% of annual language-service revenue.
Software publishers, media-production or dubbing companies, language schools, individual freelancers, and internal language departments not primarily classified in NAICS 541930.
alternatives.rationale: The dataset estimates 28 firms in the target band. Applying its 15.65% EBITDA margin maps that band to approximately $6.39 million-$63.90 million of revenue. In the disclosed 2024 Nimdzi U.S. cohort within that revenue interval, seven providers are described as translation/localization-led, six as interpreting-led, and one as mixed. Scaling the observed 7:6:1 composition to 28 gives 14 translation/localization-led firms. The low endpoint is the seven observed providers; the high endpoint allows all 14 unclassified band firms except the minimum six interpreting-led and one mixed provider to be translation/localization-led. The ALC U.S. survey's 55% translation and 42% interpreting revenue split supports both models being material but does not identify firm counts.

alternatives.name: Interpreting-led language-service provider
inclusion_criteria: U.S. business classified in NAICS 541930 and estimated to have $1 million to $10 million of EBITDA.
More than 50% of annual language-service revenue is generated by live spoken-language or sign-language interpreting delivered onsite, by telephone, by video, or for conferences.
The business may also provide written translation or localization, but live interpreting remains the majority revenue model.
exclusion_criteria: Businesses with more than 50% of annual language-service revenue from written translation or localization.
Balanced providers for which neither live interpreting nor written translation/localization exceeds 50% of annual language-service revenue.
Captioning-only, telecommunications relay, software, staffing, training, or internal language-access operations not primarily classified in NAICS 541930.
alternatives.rationale: Six disclosed U.S. providers in the Nimdzi target-revenue cohort are interpreting-led. Scaling the observed 7:6:1 composition to the dataset's 28-firm band gives 12 interpreting-led firms. The low endpoint is the six observed providers; the high endpoint allows all 14 unclassified band firms except the minimum seven translation/localization-led and one mixed provider to be interpreting-led. The range preserves the possibility that interpreting-focused providers are the true largest target-band archetype.

residual.name: Balanced, other-service-led, or unclassifiable NAICS 541930 providers
residual.rationale: The residual includes providers for which neither written translation/localization nor live interpreting exceeds 50% of language-service revenue, providers led by another in-scope service, and firms lacking a defensible revenue split. One mixed provider is observed in the 14-company target-revenue cohort; scaling gives a base of two. A high of six allows substantial classification nonresponse while retaining at least 22 of 28 firms in the two enumerated operating models.

sources.title: 2022 SUSB Annual Data Tables by Establishment Industry
NAICS 541930 Translation and Interpretation Services definition
NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54193: Translation and Interpretation Services
Review of the ALC 2015 Industry Survey

target_archetype.selection_basis: The 28-firm target-band total is fixed from the normalized dataset. Its 15.65% margin assumption implies a revenue screen of about $6.39 million-$63.90 million. The publicly disclosed 2024 Nimdzi ranking and just-below-ranking cohort contains 14 U.S. or U.S./China providers in that interval: seven described as translation/localization-led (MotionPoint, PGLS, Unbabel, Acclaro, Lilt, Willingjet, and Terra Translations), six described as interpreting-led (Certified Languages International, Linguava Interpreters, Hanna Interpreting Services, Language Link Corp, Translations in Motion, and Interpreters Unlimited Group), and one mixed provider (Akorbi). Scaling 7:6:1 to 28 produces base counts of 14, 12, and 2, so translation-localization-led is selected by the largest base count. The ranking is a non-random, incomplete provider list, includes verified and estimated revenue, and does not prove each firm's NAICS assignment or EBITDA; consequently the supported count ranges overlap and the selection remains uncertain.

value_basis.scope: Translation/localization-led NAICS 541930 providers in the estimated $1 million-$10 million EBITDA band
value_basis.controllable_value_added_boundary: For this archetype, controllable value added would equal recognized customer revenue less only empirically documented third-party amounts that are contractually passed through to the customer, separately invoiced or otherwise demonstrably agent-like, and outside the operator's control. Freelance linguist and interpreter purchases are not presumed pass-through: they remain controllable contractor cash cost unless target-specific principal-versus-agent and cost-control evidence proves otherwise. Because no target-band reconciliation identifies qualifying pass-through amounts, R_cva and its employee/controllable-contractor cash-cost components are MISSING.
value_basis.rationale: No defensible target-archetype value basis is available. The dataset's 41.78% labor share measures broad-NAICS employee compensation against receipts and omits separately purchased freelance linguist and other contractor services. Its OEWS role mix is a 5419 proxy dominated by veterinary occupations and is invalid for this archetype. The ALC survey reports that 89% of translation/interpreting work was performed by freelancers and a median of 120 independent contractors, but it does not report target-band employee cash cost, controllable contractor cash cost, revenue pass-through, or mutually exclusive role cash-cost weights. Those facts cannot support employee or controllable-contractor basis identifiers or bounded role weights, so the basis remains missing.
caveats: Do not use any veterinary occupation from the dataset's 4-digit 5419 OEWS mix.
Do not treat employee payroll share as total controllable cash cost in a contractor-intensive provider model.
Do not infer revenue pass-through, controllable contractor cost, or mutually exclusive role weights from worker counts, task incidence, or broad-industry occupation shares.
Selection uncertainty requires any future target-specific basis to encompass plausible reclassification to the overlapping interpreting-led archetype.

source_hints.source: Target-band provider income statements, customer invoices, vendor ledgers, and quality-of-earnings schedules that reconcile gross billings, linguist purchases, reimbursables, and net revenue
source_hints.why: Needed to distinguish provider revenue from pass-through purchases and establish a target-specific denominator.

source_hints.source: Target-band provider financial statements, confidential management accounts, lender quality-of-earnings reports, or a representative Association of Language Companies cost survey separating employee compensation and controllable freelance-linguist purchases
source_hints.why: Needed to construct employee and controllable-contractor cash-cost components and mutually exclusive role cash-cost weights.

source_hints.source: Named translation/localization providers, workflow-system vendors, and audited case studies with dates, baseline measures, adoption cohorts, and realized operating results
source_hints.why: Needed to establish target-specific timing, scope, and realization rather than relying on cross-industry claims.

source_hints.source: Customer contracts, procurement records, provider disclosures, and buyer surveys segmented to translation/localization services and target-sized providers
source_hints.why: Needed to measure retained price, volume, service mix, and customer behavior.

source_hints.source: Closed U.S. language-service transactions from buyer announcements, SEC filings, court records, GF Data, DealStats, or specialist transaction advisers, with revenue mix and deal dates
source_hints.why: Needed to measure access to comparable sellers and entry/exit multiples for translation/localization-led providers.

source_hints.source: Longitudinal target-provider operating data, customer language-access requirements, contract service levels, and comparable provider restructurings
source_hints.why: Needed to determine the minimum human operating organization and whether the modeled provider remains viable at year five.

controllable_value_added: What target-provider evidence reconciles gross billings to net revenue and separately identifies linguist, interpreter, platform, reimbursable, and other pass-through amounts?
Which costs are principal-account revenue inputs versus agent-like pass-throughs, and what base, low, and high shares are supported for the selected archetype and overlapping alternative?
What target-band evidence supports separate employee and controllable-contractor cash-cost amounts as shares of the appropriate revenue denominator?
Can mutually exclusive role cash-cost weights be reconciled to those amounts without using the contaminated 4-digit 5419 occupation mix?
operational_realization: What target-archetype evidence supports each annual realization endpoint over five years, including dependencies, customer approvals, workflow coverage, and failure cases?
How do documented schedules differ between written translation/localization providers and the overlapping interpreting-led alternative?
commercial_retention: What contemporaneous target-provider or buyer evidence supports base, low, and high commercial-retention values, separately identifying price, volume, service-mix, and customer-switching effects?
Are the supported ranges robust across regulated, legal, life-sciences, technical, and general-business customer mixes?
sale_availability: What share of comparable U.S. translation/localization-led providers was credibly offered for sale over a defined recent period, and what population and size bridge maps that evidence to this target band?
Does the supported sale-availability range remain valid under the overlapping interpreting-led classification?
buyer_access_win: What share of comparable U.S. translation/localization-led providers was credibly accessible to a buyer over a defined recent period, and what population and size bridge maps that evidence to this target band?
Among accessible sellers, what target-specific evidence supports the probability that a qualified buyer wins a signed transaction, separately from seller availability?
Do the supported buyer-access and win-rate ranges remain valid under the overlapping interpreting-led classification?
valuation_robustness: What closed comparable transactions support entry and downside-exit multiples for target-sized translation/localization-led providers, with date, revenue mix, size, growth, and margin bridges disclosed?
How sensitive are comparable multiples to customer concentration, contractor dependence, regulated vertical exposure, and translation-versus-interpreting revenue mix?
operator_survival: What target-specific evidence establishes the minimum year-five roles, headcount, capabilities, controls, and service-level obligations required for a viable translation/localization-led operator?
Can every claimed removable role be reconciled against required project management, linguistic quality, vendor management, security, sales, finance, and executive-accountability functions?

special_notes: This record describes only the selected translation/localization-led archetype and must not be presented as the score of all NAICS 541930 businesses.
Selection uncertainty is true because the two largest archetype ranges overlap; downstream readiness must remain UNPROVEN.
The value basis is MISSING. Do not substitute the contaminated 4-digit OEWS role mix, and never use veterinary SOCs.
The target-band count and provider-model allocation are estimates, not observed EBITDA-level firm counts.

Willingjet is reported as United States/China in the source; moving it out of the U.S. cohort would make the observed translation/localization-led and interpreting-led counts equal, reinforcing selection uncertainty.
Archetype assignment is based on majority language-service revenue, not the mere presence of a listed service.
